title: Airplane Wall Decor Shelf
summary: A wall-mounted shelf shaped like a biplane, with a propeller that actually spins and hole-sawed wheels on the landing gear.
description:
This is a small shelf with a lot of personality: the fuselage doubles as the mounting board, the wings are the shelves themselves, and the nose gets a round wooden face with a propeller that spins on its mounting screw. Kids' room decor lives or dies on details like a propeller that really turns, so don't overtighten that center screw — snug enough to hold, loose enough to spin freely.

A hole saw does double duty here, cutting both the round face piece and the wheels, so if you're buying one for this project, get a kit with both a 4" and a 2" bit. No hole saw kit is a fine substitute too — craft-store wood circles work for both the face and the wheels.

tools.note: 4" bit for the face, 2" bit for the wheels — or substitute craft-store wood circles for either.

materials.name: 1-1/4" brad nails or self-tapping wood screws

steps.title: Attach the shelves
steps.body: Attach the 1x4 wing supports flush to the back of the fuselage board, set in 3" from the outsides. Predrill and use glue.

steps.title: Attach the front
steps.body: Attach the second fuselage board centered on the shelves, inset 3/4" from the shelf fronts. Predrill, glue, and use 2" screws.

steps.title: Cut the propeller face
steps.body: Cut the round face out with a 4" hole saw and position it on the nose of the plane — the hole saw will have already bored a small center hole for the propeller screw.

steps.title: Attach the propeller
steps.body: Attach the propeller to the face and screw it down through its center, through the face, and into the fuselage front — snug enough to hold, loose enough that it still spins freely.

steps.title: Build the landing gear
steps.body: Build the landing gear by joining the 1x2 pieces with brad nails and wood glue.

steps.title: Cut and attach the wheels
steps.body: Cut the wheels with a 2" hole saw and attach to the wheel base. Craft-store round wood circles work as a substitute if you don't have a hole saw kit.

steps.title: Attach the landing gear and wheels
steps.body: Attach the landing gear to the bottom of the plane, inset 2" from the front, with 1-1/4" screws and glue.
materials: 1-1/4" brad nails or self-tapping wood screws

steps.title: Finish and hang
steps.body: Fill any visible holes with wood filler and finish as desired. Attach a picture hanging cleat in two places on the back and hang with drywall anchors.

images.alt: A wall-mounted shelf shaped like a biplane with a spinning propeller and wheeled landing gear
